coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical impedance sensor for herbicide alachlor based on imprinted polymer receptor
coredata.dc:creator: Elshafey, Reda
Radi, Abd-Elgawad
coredata.dc:description: Alachlor (ALA), a carcinogenic herbicide is detected in surface and groundwater, as well as in milk, eggs, and meat. So, the sensitive detection of ALA is essential for humans. To respond, we report a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) receptor for simple electrochemical sensing of ALA. The MIP film was immobilized onto glassy carbon electrode by electrodeposition of o‑phenylenediamine (o‑PD) in the presence of ALA as a template, followed by the ALA leaching out to create specific recognition cavities to ALA. The stepwise fabrication of the sensor was characterized by recording cyclic, square wave voltammetry peak currents and the electron transfer resistance (Ret) where the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was used as an electro-active probe. The mole ratio of ALA to o‑PD as well as the controlled release of the alachlor from the MIP‑o‑PD network was optimized to create a maximum number of free imprinted sites that affected the sensor sensitivity and its linear range. The increment in the Ret of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− upon selective recognition of ALA to the free cavities of MIP film is employed as the sensor response. At the optimal experimental conditions, the relative change in Ret is linearly correlated to the ALA concentrations in a range of 1 nM to 1 μM ALA. This sensor shows the high sensitivity of ALA with a limit of detection of 0.78 nM ALA. The affinity of MIP to ALA is proved by calculating the dissociation constant (Kd) in nM range. The high selectivity of the MIP sensor against the interferences propachlor and metalaxyl has been confirmed. Finally, the reliability of MIP-GCE for ALA detection is proved in tap water samples spiked by ALA. These findings indicate that such a sensor can be used for in-field measurements of other herbicides as a simple detection platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alachlor
Impedance
Molecular imprinted polymer
Sensor
o‑Phenylenediamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of ALA using MIP film
Application of MIP sensor for ALA detection
Analysis of electrochemical ALA/MIP binding parameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms of o‑phenylenediamine (o‑PD) electropolymerisation at a glassy carbon electrode for (A) NIP (40 μM o‑PD) and (B) MIP (40 μM o‑PD + 20 μM Alachlor) in acetate buffer (pH 5.2), 50 mV/s, 20 cycles.

(A), (B) Cyclic voltammograms and Nyquist plots recorded at bare glassy carbon electrode (curve a), MIP and NIP-modified (curves b, c) and after ALA removal (curve d) electrodes from 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in phosphate buffer pH 7. Inset of (A): Square wave voltammograms are recorded at the same conditions.

(A) Comparison of Ret recorded at GCE after the MIP electropolymerization, treatment with EtOH/alkaline and the MeOH/Acetic acid solutions. (B) Effect of the ALA template to o‑PD mole ratio on the MIP sensor responses recorded at ALA concentration of 10, 100, 500 nM.

(A) Impedance sensing of Alachlor–MIP interactions. Typical EIS responses recorded at the MIP-modified GCE at the increasing concentrations of ALA: 0.0 nM to 1 μM. (B) Calibration plot corresponding to (ΔR/R0) % vs. ALA (nM), inset is the linear relation of (ΔR/R0) % vs. Log ALA conc. (nM), the error bar is the SD of average of three independent measurements.

The plot of ΔR/R0 vs. ALA concentration (nM), the full line is the fit according to Langmuir isotherm (see the text). Inset: the linearized adsorption isotherm of ALA recognition to MIP sensor surface. The line is the best linear fit to the experimental data from which the dissociation constant (Kd) was determined.
The MIP–ALA sensor response ΔR/R0% recognition against 10 and 50 nM of MTX, POP and ALA, respectively, the error bars represent the SD of three measurements.
Schematic illustration for the fabrication of the MIP/o-PD/GCE sensor.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical impedance sensor for herbicide alachlor based on imprinted polymer receptor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Damietta University, Damietta 34517, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alachlor (ALA), a carcinogenic herbicide is detected in surface and groundwater, as well as in milk, eggs, and meat. So, the sensitive detection of ALA is essential for humans. To respond, we report a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) receptor for simple electrochemical sensing of ALA. The MIP film was immobilized onto glassy carbon electrode by electrodeposition of o‑phenylenediamine (o‑PD) in the presence of ALA as a template, followed by the ALA leaching out to create specific recognition cavities to ALA. The stepwise fabrication of the sensor was characterized by recording cyclic, square wave voltammetry peak currents and the electron transfer resistance (Ret) where the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was used as an electro-active probe. The mole ratio of ALA to o‑PD as well as the controlled release of the alachlor from the MIP‑o‑PD network was optimized to create a maximum number of free imprinted sites that affected the sensor sensitivity and its linear range. The increment in the Ret of the redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− upon selective recognition of ALA to the free cavities of MIP film is employed as the sensor response. At the optimal experimental conditions, the relative change in Ret is linearly correlated to the ALA concentrations in a range of 1 nM to 1 μM ALA. This sensor shows the high sensitivity of ALA with a limit of detection of 0.78 nM ALA. The affinity of MIP to ALA is proved by calculating the dissociation constant (Kd) in nM range. The high selectivity of the MIP sensor against the interferences propachlor and metalaxyl has been confirmed. Finally, the reliability of MIP-GCE for ALA detection is proved in tap water samples spiked by ALA. These findings indicate that such a sensor can be used for in-field measurements of other herbicides as a simple detection platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their well-defined toxicity for humans and animals, the misuse of herbicides is leading to severe environmental problems, such as the pollution of rivers, lakes and underground water. This concern is motivating the research for developing sensitive and straightforward methods to monitor herbicide residues [1–4]. Alachlor (ALA) or so-called (2‑chloro‑N‑[2,6‑diethylphenyl]‑N‑methoxymethyl‑acetanilide), is an emergent herbicide in many countries around the world. ALA has been widely used for grass and weed control in corn, potatoes, maize, rice, soybeans, and sunflower crops [5–7]. ALA has been confirmed as a carcinogen for humans (class B2) [6] as well as showing an oncogenic effect in rats and mice according to the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) studies [8–10]. These toxic effects have grown a public concern to establish easy to use methods to detect ALA at low levels [7].

ALA has frequently been detected in rain, surface, and groundwater [11,12] in the low parts-per-billion range. ALA has also been detected in milk, eggs, and meat when the livestock and poultry feed contaminated water or feed. The ALA maximum level of 2 μg/L and 0.1 μg/L in drinking water has been established by the EPA and the European Union, respectively [2]. The trace analysis of ALA is significant in environmental testing programs. That is not only due to its toxic effects, but also the high toxicity of ALA metabolites in comparison to their parent molecule [13].

Several analytical methods have been reported to analyze alachlor, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)–mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) [14–16], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [17], solid phase extraction coupled to HPLC-UV analysis [18]. The electrochemical behavior of alachlor has also been utilized by studying the polarographic reduction in aqueous solutions on a hanging mercury electrode [19,20]. Furthermore, some Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) have been established for ALA detection [21–24].

The stability and the cost of antibodies as well as the lack of simplicity, and the need for qualified personnel of chromatographic methods do not satisfy the requirements for the on-site application. These constraints may be overcome by replacing the natural antibodies with stable and cheap synthetic analogs, molecularly imprinted polymers MIPs.

MIPs are synthesized by polymerization of functional monomers in the presence of a template analyte followed by leaching out the template. Through which, cavities complementary in size, shape, and functionality to the target analyte will be created. These designed imprinted sites exhibit high affinity to the template molecule over other structurally related compounds and enhance the selectivity against the complex matrices [25]. Molecular printing by electrochemical deposition has been initiated in recent years [26–28] to overcome the bulk polymerization drawbacks of integration of MIP onto the transducer's surface in addition to the time consuming and slow template-to-MIP binding kinetics.

Various nanomaterials have shown interest to be integrated with the MIP layer for sensitive electrochemical detection and wider range of detection for various analytes [28]. Among them carbon nitride nanotubes [29] or graphene quantum dots decorated carbon nitride nanotubes [30–32], and platinum nanoparticles [33]. Graphene/graphene oxide or graphene quantum dots have been employed to form composites with gold nanoparticles [34], silver nanoparticles [35], platinum nanoparticles [36], and palladium nanoparticles [37] for sensing tyrosine and mycotoxins, ochratoxin A and citrinin, respectively. Finally, multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified with Fe@Au nanoparticles and platinum nanoparticles are also used for the detection of cefexime in human plasma and simazine, respectively [38,39].

Although few MIP based sensors are reported for herbicides such as atrazine [4], dimethoate [40], this is the first reported trial of MIP based electrochemical sensor of alachlor. In the paper, we report a simple herbicide alachlor sensor based on molecularly imprinted electro-synthesized o‑phenylene diamine (o‑PD) as functional monomer and alachlor as a template. Electrodeposition of o‑PD film was carried out by applying cyclic voltammetry (20 scans) at scan rate 50 mV/s from ALA/o‑PD concentration ratio, 1:2 in acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 5.2) on a glassy carbon surface. The mole ratio of ALA template to the o‑PD monomer and an efficient removal of the template from the polymeric matrix are optimized to obtain stable MIP with a high number of complementary sites to the template. ALA: o‑PD mole ratio of (1:5, 1:4, 1:3 and 1:2) is tested. The extraction of the ALA target molecule was also optimized by utilizing two treatments, ethanol/water/NaOH and methanol/acetic acid (9:1 v/v%). The latter solution mixture has efficiently removed the ALA from the polymeric network, leaving specific cavities for ALA rebinding. The MIP film is subsequently utilized for fabrication of impedance-based sensor of ALA. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to characterize the o‑PD electrodeposition as well as the template removal and ALA recognition to the sensor surface in the presence of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe. The increase in the electron transfer resistance (Ret) of ferro/ferricyanide redox probe is recorded upon the binding of ALA to the MIP-sensor surface. The sensor response was employed to calculate the dissociation constant as an indicator to the MIP sensor affinity and selective recognition to ALA. The imprinting efficiency of the prepared MIP was evaluated by specific recognition of alachlor against its structurally related molecules such as propachlor and metalaxyl. The ALA-MIP sensor has been applied for the detection of tap water spiked with ALA and the results indicated high recovery and sensitivity.
Alachlor, Propachlor, Metalaxyl, o‑Phenylenediamine (o‑PD, 98%), potassium ferricyanide K3[Fe(CN)6], potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6] were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA.

The standard herbicides stock solutions were prepared by dissolving in methanol and stored at 4 °C. The working solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solution with the appropriate 0.20 M phosphate buffer solution. An equimolar of K3[Fe(CN)6])/K4[Fe(CN)6] (2 mM) was prepared in phosphate buffer pH 7.2 (0.2 M). Glacial acetic acid and sodium acetate were used to obtain the acetate buffer pH 5.2 (0.2 M). The freshly made solutions of o‑PD were obtained by dissolving the appropriate amount in acetate buffer pH 5.2 (0.2 M). The phosphate buffer solution pH 7 was made from KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4, with distilled water. Other chemicals used were of analytical grade, used without further purification. All other reagents were of analytical grade and solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ/cm2). All reagents are used without further purification.

Electrochemical measurements of cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using a PGSTAT 302 N Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (Eco-Chemie, The Netherlands) controlled by Nova 1.11 software. A traditional three-electrode system was used for all measurements; a MIP-modified glassy carbon (GCE) electrode (2 mm diameter) as the working electrode, a Pt wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode and an Ag|AgCl|KCl (3 M KCl) electrode as a reference electrode were used. CV experiments were conducted at a scan rate of 100 mV/s and swept at −0.2 V to 1.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode). SWV measurements were carried out under the following conditions: the voltage was scanned from −0.2 V to 1.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode) with a step potential of 5 mV, the amplitude, and the frequency were kept as 20 mV and 25 Hz, respectively. The impedance spectra were recorded over the frequency range of 100 kHz–0.01 Hz at a bias potential of 0.23 V and amplitude of 10 mV. The impedance data were represented in the complex impedance (Nyquist plot), and the Nova 1.11 software was used to analyze the impedance spectra using an appropriate equivalent electrical circuit.

Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface was polished before modification by using 1.0 and 0.05 μm wet alumina slurry followed by ultrasonication in distilled water to remove any adsorbed alumina particles. Then, the electrode was subjected to cyclic voltammetry sweeps between 0.2 and 1.5 V in 0.5 M H2SO4 until a stable cyclic voltammogram was obtained. Electropolymerization of the o‑PD film was achieved by cyclic voltammetry (20 scans) in the potential range of 0.0–1.1 V at a scan rate 50 mV/s from a solution containing 40 μM o‑PD and alachlor, 20 μM in acetate buffer solution, pH 5.2 (0.2 M). A control electrode modified with non- imprinted polymer (NIP) was obtained in the same way, but without ALA being added as a template. Two treatment conditions have been used to extract the alachlor from the MIP polymeric network. First, the o‑PD MIP modified GCE was immersed in ethanol-water (2:1, v/v) solution containing 0.25 M NaOH and stirring for 15 min, followed by washing with water. While the second treatment was using a solution mixture of methanol: acetic acid (9:1 V/V) under stirring for 20 min, followed by washing with distilled water. The stepwise formation of o‑PD-MIP/GCE senor is represented in the Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of ALA using MIP film
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition of ALA to MIP-sensor surface has been studied by incubating the MIP modified GCE in phosphate buffer, pH 7.2 containing a various concentration of ALA for 30 min. After the washing with water to remove any loosely bound molecules, CV, SWV and EIS were recorded from a solution of 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−/phosphate buffer pH 7 (0.2 M). Control measurements were also performed with NIP modified electrode without ALA.

For specificity testing, structurally related compounds, herbicide propachlor, and fungicide mataxaxyl were employed as either template for MIP formation or analyte for incubating with ALA-MIP film. Metalaxyl-MIP-modified GCE was dipped into various solutions of ALA for 30 min, washed with distilled water to remove the unbound molecules. ALA/MIP sensor film was also used to test the cross-reactivity against propachlor and metalaxyl.

Typical cyclic voltammograms recorded during the electropolymerization of o‑PD in the absence (NIP) and the presence of ALA (MIP) on the glassy carbon electrode, respectively are shown (Fig. 1 A, B). Two anodic peaks are recorded, with no reduction peaks, showing the irreversibility of the electropolymerization process. On the subsequent cycles, the peak current decreased, indicating that the electrode surface is covered with a dense polymer film of low conductivity as it hindered further o‑PD monomer oxidation [41,42]. The amount of charge passed during the formation of NIP formation is higher than in the case of MIP is shown; this is due to the presence of the ALA that occupies sites on the GCE surface during the MIP and not in NIP.

Cyclic, square wave voltammograms and impedance spectra have been recorded to characterize the MIP electropolymerization films before and after template removal (Fig. 2 ). As displayed, (Fig. 2A, curve b), the CV peak current suppressed as well as the peak separation enhanced at the MIP film modified GCE for the bare GCE (Fig. 2A, curve a). This is because the formation of the electro-inactive film completely covered the surface of the modified electrode and hindered redox probe access to the electrode surface [43]. The same trend in the SWV peak current is shown (Fig. 2A, inset). The impedance spectra are also consistent with the CV and SWV results (Fig. 2B). However, for the NIP (non-imprinted molecular polymer with no ALA), high CV, SWV peak currents are measured (i.e., high conductivity of the layer in comparison to MIP) (Fig. 2, curve c). Such behavior is supported by recording a small radius of the EIS semicircle (Fig. 2B, curve c). Altogether are confirming the occupation of ALA to polymeric network.

EIS data was represented by Nyquist plots and consist of a high-frequency semicircle and straight line at low-frequency range. The semicircle portion reflects the electron transfer kinetics, while the straight line represents the diffusion of the redox molecules from the bulk solution to/from the electrode surface.

The impedance spectra were interpreted by using Randle's equivalent circuit (Fig. 2B, inset). This circuit model includes a solution resistance in series with the diffusion part (charge transfer resistance and Warburg impedance), and in parallel with constant phase element instead of double layer capacitance because of the non-uniform distribution of capacitance over the electrode surface.
As the MIP between o-PD and the ALA is now confirmed, the interaction between ALA and the polymerized moieties of o-PD involve the mutual hydrogen-bonding [44] as illustrated in Scheme 1.

The extraction of ALA from the imprinted film is a crucial parameter for the sensor sensitivity as well as the linear range of detection. Two conditions were performed to remove template ALA from the imprinted poly‑o‑PD film deposited on the GCE surface. The first washing step was to use ethanol/alkaline mixture [45], while the second was the use of methanol/acetic acid (9:1 v/v%) [4]. The treated MIP-GCE surfaces with the removal solution were also characterized electrochemically (Fig. 2). When the MIP-film is treated with methanol/acetic acid (9:1 v/v%), typical CV and SWV redox behavior of the ferri/ferrocyanide was recovered. This response is confirming the elution of the highest number ALA molecules from the polymer film, leaving cavities and the probe has access to the electrode surface (Fig. 2A, B curves d). Such finding was supported by recording SWV peak current value of 1.47 μA versus the 0.106 μA at the MIP deposition on GCE (Fig. 2A, inset). Small radius semicircle of the Nyquist plot at Ret of 159 kΩ is also measured to the Ret at the MIP formation at GCE of value 1290 kΩ (Fig. 3A). So, the methanol/acetic acid treatment was efficient to leach out the ALA from the polymeric network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MIP sensor for ALA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the MIP sensor for ALA detection is affected by the mole ratio of o-PD monomer and ALA template during electropolymerization process. In this work, the molar ratio of template ALA to monomer o‑PD of (1:5, 1:4, 1:3, and 1:2) was changed followed by the leaching out of ALA by incubating in MeOH/AcOH (9:1 v/v%).

These MIPs have been tested for the detection of ALA (10, 100, and 500 nM). The relative change in Ret values at MIP-sensors fabricated from a different ratio of ALA and o‑PD are shown in (Fig. 3B). As depicted, the maximum response of MIP (ALA: o‑PD, 1:2) is recorded for all measured ALA concentrations in comparison to MIP of the remaining mole ratio of ALA: o‑PD. This is due to the maximum recognition sites at the MIP of 1:2 ratio, produce the highest sensor signal. While, at the ratio higher than 1:2, the quantity of available binding sites is reduced due to the lower concentration of ALA. This finding was confirmed by the wide linear range of the ALA that has been detected by the MIP sensor (o‑PD: ALA ratio, 2:1) as sown in the next section. Moreover, the high concentration of o‑PD might lead to increase of non-selective binding sites generated by surplus o‑PD, and a decrease of the specific adsorption to ALA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of electrochemical ALA/MIP binding parameters
ce:section.ce:para: As a small molecule, ALA herbicide is rarely detected by considering its electroactive behavior; we study its rebinding to MIP sensor by recording the change in the Ret in the presence of the ferri/ferrocyanide redox probe. Quantitative analysis of ALA binding was performed with incubation of a MIP sensor in 1 nM to 1 μM ALA solution by placing the electrode for 30 min into the appropriate calibration solution followed by washing with deionised water.

Fig. 4A is shown the dose response of the ALA binding to the MIP sensors surface. Upon increasing ALA concentration, more imprinted sites were rebound with ALA, the electrode reaction of the ferro/ferricyanide probe was impeded by the binding of ALA with MIP. Due to the increasing number of binding sites in the film occupied by ALA molecules, the increase in Ret (increase in the radius of the semicircle of the Nyquist diagrams) with the rise in ALA concentration are shown. Thus, the analytical response of increase in Ret is proportional to ALA concentration.

Fig. 4B shows the concentration dependence of the electron transfer resistance Ret of the redox marker upon ALA binding to the MIP electrodes. The MIP electrode displayed a linear response in the low concentrations ranging from 1 nM to 1 μM, with a detection limit of 0.78 nM (as three times the standard deviation of the blank signal). Such low LOD is good as compared to the maximum limit of 2 μg/L and 0.1 μg/L in drinking water (EPA).
The linear regression curve was shown as ΔR/R0% = 80.86 + 227.92 ∗ Log [ALA] nM with a coefficient of correlation of 0.998.

The binding assay of ALA to MIP sensor was investigated based on the calibration data to calculate the dissociation constant; we plot the sensor responses upon selective binding to ALA of various concentrations (Fig. 5 ). As shown, there is hyperbolic curve of AlA-MIP binding that to be interpreted according to non-linear regression of the following equation as it has the shape of Langmuir isotherm.
∆ R
R 0
=
∆ R
R 0
max
C
K
d + C

From the isotherm, the Ret value increases gradually with increasing concentration of ALA and finally reaches an adsorption plateau at 500 nM due to the saturation of the binding sites in the MIP membrane with the target analyte molecules. Also, the linearized fitting of this relation could be used to obtain the same result of dissociation constant Kd = 8.3 nM ± 3.4.

On the other hand, the binding of ALA to a NIP electrode reflects the non-specific surface sorption and is poorly fitted by the Langmuir isotherm (data not shown). Since the differences between the MIP and NIP surfaces are only the presence/absence of structurally imprinted cavities on the receptor surface, the above observations are indicative of successful MIP fabrication. Considering this Kd value of 8.3 ± 0.5 nM for the MIP-sensors, we can state that the MIP-provided affinity as compared to the Ab-ALA in the ELISA immunoassays.

Selective recognition of the template molecule to the MIP sensor is an essential feature in biosensing and the analytical application of MIPs. The interfering species tested were selected among those that have similar molecular weights and are most likely to be used as herbicides or fungicide, such as propachlor (POP) and metalaxyl (MTX). Two selectivity procedures were tested; the first is investigating the ALA-MIP sensor against POP and MTX and the second is to use MTX as a template to form MIP sensor (MTX-MIP) for ALA binding studyies. Both cases, the change in the EIS signals is recorded and compared to the ALA-MIP sensor. The value of ΔR/R0% is recorded for binding of ALA, propachlor, and metalaxyl at two concentration ranges of 10, 50 nM are shown in Fig. 6 . The responses from the ALA-MIP senor toward the POP and MTX are negligible to the ALA specific recognition. Such comparison is confirming the selectivity of the MIP for ALA sensing against those molecules. Moreover, the interaction of ALA to the MTX-MIP has shown an insignificant increase in the Ret (data not shown).

As a preliminary testing of MIP sensor for real sample analysis, we employ a tap water spiked with ALA in concentrations range as in the calibration plot. The estimated recoveries from the imprinted electrodes were shown good values ranged 95.5% to 103.5% with average SD standard deviation of 4.5 to 6.2.

In summary, a novel electrochemical sensor for the determination of alachlor was designed using a molecular imprinting technique. The ALA MIP sensor was created by electropolymerization of the o‑phenylenediamine monomer onto GCE in the presence of alachlor template, followed by the removal of ALA under optimized conditions. The concentration ratio of o‑PD to ALA and the extraction conditions were investigated for optimization. The mole ratio of ALA: o‑PD (1:5), as well as the methanol/acetic acid (9:1 v/v%) solution are the optimal parameters for stable MIP layer with high number of cavities. The increase in impedance of a ferro/ferricyanide probe was used to monitor both template removal and subsequent target analyte binding. The sensor response was linear to the ALA concentration in a buffer in the range 1 nM to 1 μM with a detection limit of 0.78 nM. The developed sensor has proven to be highly selective toward ALA against structurally similar compounds at the same concentration. The affinity of MIP to ALA is proved by calculating the dissociation constant (Kd) of 8.3 nM ± 3.4 nM. Furthermore, satisfactory recovery values were obtained in the analysis of spiked water samples, demonstrating the lack of matrix effects. Further improvement in the sensitivity of ALA detection could be proposed such as the use of various nanomaterials (metal nanoparticles and carbon nanostructures) in the modification of MIP sensor. The electrochemical behavior of ALA could be combined to the MIP and used for its detection, where the MIP cavities could work as filling pores for sensitive reduction of ALA electrochemically.
There are no conflicts to declare.
